revision.text:
{{Expand Japanese|topic=cult|カミムスビ|date=October 2021}} {{Infobox deity | type = Japanese | name = Kamimusubi | image = Japanese Primordial Deities-en.svg | caption = Creation of the world according to the {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]}}, showing the five primordial gods (''[[kotoamatsukami]]'') and the subsequent seven generations of deities (''[[kamiyonanayo]]'') | god_of = ''[[Kotoamatsukami]]'' | script_name = Japanese | script = 神産巣日神 | other_names = Kamimusuhi, Kamumusubi | cult_center = | symbols = | consort = | parents = None; self-generated | siblings = | children = * [[Ame-no-Koyane]] * {{ill|Kisagaihime and Umugihime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ}} * [[Sukunabikona]] * Amenoikutama | texts = {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]}}, ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', ''Izumo-no-kuni Fudoki'' }} '''Kamimusubi''' (神産巣日), also known as '''Kamimusuhi''' among other variants, is a ''[[kami]]'' and [[god of creation]] in [[Japanese mythology]]. They are a ''[[hitorigami]],'' and the third of the first three ''kami'' to come into existence (''[[Kotoamatsukami]]''),{{Cite book |last=Gibney |first=Frank |title=Britannica International Encyclopedia |publisher=TBS-BRITANNICA |oclc=704151705}} alongside [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] and [[Takamimusubi]], forming a trio at the beginning of all creation.{{Cite book |last1=Breen |first1=John |url=https://www.taylorfrancis.com/books/mono/10.4324/9781315027890/shinto-history-john-breen-mark-teeuwen |title=Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami |last2=Teeuwen |first2=Mark |year=2013 |pages=55 |doi=10.4324/9781315027890|isbn=9781136826979 }} The name is composed of kami, denoting deity, and musubi, meaning "effecting force of creation". == Mythology == At the time of the [[Japanese creation myth|creation of heaven and earth]], Kamimusubi was in [[Takamagahara]] next to [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] and [[Takamimusubi]]. In {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]}}, Kamimusubi is an ancestral god who sits in Takamagahara and assists the gods of Izumo, and is called "Mi-Oya" (honorfic name for ancestor) by other gods.{{Cite book |last=Miura |first=Sukeyuki |title=Fudoki no Sekai『風土記の世界』 |publisher=Iwanamishoten |year=2016 |isbn=9784004316046 |location=Japan |pages=161–177}} Kamimusubi became the ancestral god of the five grains (progenitors) after Kamimusubi transformed the grains produced from the body of [[Ukemochi|Ōgetsu-hime]], the goddess of food, who was killed by [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]], the god of storms.{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9381 |archive-url= |access-date=2021-09-24 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}} At the beginning of the text, it is said that Kamimusubi is a genderless ''[[hitorigami]]'', but when [[Ōkuninushi]] is killed by [[:ja:八十神|Yasogami]](八十神), his mother, [[:ja:刺国若比売|Sashikuniwakahime]](刺国若比売), makes a request to Kamimusubi. However, here, Kamimusubi is considered a [[goddess]] because Kisagaihime and Umugihime were healed by "mother's milk". Since the Izumo gods are not mentioned in ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', Kamimusubi exists only as a kinematic pair of Takamimusubi. In ''Izumo-no-kuni Fudoki'', Kamimusubi appears as the ancestor of the ''Tochi-gami'' (land gods) who appear as the origin of the name of [[:ja:島根半島|Shimane Peninsula]] . Many of the ''Tochi-gami,'' such as Kisagaihime and Umugihime, are goddesses, and it is thought that Kamimusubi is the mother goddess in the genealogy of a [[Matriarchy|matriarchal society]]. Kamimusubi is considered in many versions to be the mother of [[Sukunabikona]]. {{Cite book |last=Ashkenazi |first=Michael |title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology |publisher=ABC-CLIO |year=2003 |isbn=9781576074671}} {{Page needed|date=August 2021}} Sukunabikona would later assist [[Ōkuninushi]] with the development of the land. While being an ''[[Amatsukami]]'' ('Kami of heaven')'','' Kamimusubi has a strong connection to ''[[Kunitsukami]]'' ('Kami of land') in [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] tradition. Because of this, it is also theorized that Kamimusubi was a kami of the Izumo clan. == Analysis == In the early Heian period, [[Kogo Shūi]] describes Kamimusubi as the ancestor of the Ki clan. During the medieval and early modern periods, [[Motoori Norinaga]] in his [[Kojiki-den|commentary on the ''Kojiki'']] wrote that Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] are the “First Ancestors of heaven and earth, of the ''kami'', and of all existence”,Sasaki, Kiyoshi. "Amenominakanushi no Kami in Late Tokugawa Period Kokugaku". ''Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics''. Kokugakuin University. Retrieved 2022-12-03. placing greater importance on them over [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]]. This viewpoint challenged the belief of the Watari Priesthood, which was that [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] was more important than Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] because they were created first. His reasoning is that Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] are the two ''[[kami]]'' of production, and thus:
Each and every thing in the world, from heaven and earth to its manifold beings and phenomena, one and all, came to be from the productive spirit [''musubi'' ] of these two great ''kami'' of production, so that even though manifold ''kami'' are in the world, it is these [two] ''kami'' that are particularly esteemed for the blessed virtue [''mimegumi''] of their productive spirit. It is they, of all others, which should be worshiped with highest esteem.
[[Hirata Atsutane]] describes Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] in a similar way, as the "ultimate natural ancestral ''kami'' of human beings", portraying them as "our great natural parent deities", giving birth to [[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]]. == Genealogy == There are no records of a spouse for Kamimusubi, but several children are mentioned. === Children === *[[Ame-no-Koyane]]{{Cite web|first=|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto: Kami in Classic Texts: Amenokoyane|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=37|access-date=2021-03-27|website=Kokugakuin University|publisher=Establishment of a National Learning Institute for the Dissemination of Research on Shinto and Japanese Culture}}{{Cite book|last=Tanaka|title=Kashima Daiguji Keizu, Shokeifu「鹿嶋大宮司系図」『諸系譜』|volume=1|location=Japan}} *{{ill|Kisagaihime and Umugihime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ}} * [[Sukunabikona]] *Amenoikutama ===Family tree=== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} == See also == * [[Musuhi]] * [[Family tree of Japanese deities]] == References == {{Japanese mythology}} [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Japanese mythology]] [[Category:Japanese goddesses]] [[Category:Amatsukami]]

However, here, Kamimusubi is considered a [[goddess]] because Kisagaihime and Umugihime were healed by "mother's milk". In ''Izumo-no-kuni Fudoki'', Kamimusubi appears as the ancestor of the ''Tochi-gami'' (land gods) who appear as the origin of the name of [[:ja:島根半島|Shimane Peninsula]] . Kamimusubi is considered in many versions to be the mother of [[Sukunabikona]]. {{Cite book |last=Ashkenazi |first=Michael |title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology |publisher=ABC-CLIO |year=2003 |isbn=9781576074671}} {{Page needed|date=August 2021}} Sukunabikona would later assist [[Ōkuninushi]] with the development of the land. Retrieved 2022-12-03. placing greater importance on them over [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]]. This viewpoint challenged the belief of the Watari Priesthood, which was that [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] was more important than Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] because they were created first. Many of the ''Tochi-gami,'' such as Kisagaihime and Umugihime, are goddesses, and it is thought that Kamimusubi is the mother goddess in the genealogy of a [[Matriarchy|matriarchal society]]. Kamimusubi is considered in many versions to be the mother of [[Sukunabikona]]. {{Cite book |last=Ashkenazi |first=Michael |title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology |publisher=ABC-CLIO |year=2003 |isbn=9781576074671}} {{Page needed|date=August 2021}} Sukunabikona would later assist [[Ōkuninushi]] with the development of the land. While being an ''[[Amatsukami]]'' ('Kami of heaven')'','' Kamimusubi has a strong connection to ''[[Kunitsukami]]'' ('Kami of land') in [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] tradition. Because of this, it is also theorized that Kamimusubi was a kami of the Izumo clan. == Analysis == In the early Heian period, [[Kogo Shūi]] describes Kamimusubi as the ancestor of the Ki clan. During the medieval and early modern periods, [[Motoori Norinaga]] in his [[Kojiki-den|commentary on the ''Kojiki'']] wrote that Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] are the “First Ancestors of heaven and earth, of the ''kami'', and of all existence”,Sasaki, Kiyoshi. "Amenominakanushi no Kami in Late Tokugawa Period Kokugaku". ''Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics''. Kokugakuin University. Retrieved 2022-12-03. placing greater importance on them over [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]]. This viewpoint challenged the belief of the Watari Priesthood, which was that [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] was more important than Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] because they were created first. His reasoning is that Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] are the two ''[[kami]]'' of production, and thus:
Each and every thing in the world, from heaven and earth to its manifold beings and phenomena, one and all, came to be from the productive spirit [''musubi'' ] of these two great ''kami'' of production, so that even though manifold ''kami'' are in the world, it is these [two] ''kami'' that are particularly esteemed for the blessed virtue [''mimegumi''] of their productive spirit. It is they, of all others, which should be worshiped with highest esteem.
[[Hirata Atsutane]] describes Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] in a similar way, as the "ultimate natural ancestral ''kami'' of human beings", portraying them as "our great natural parent deities", giving birth to [[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]]. == Genealogy == There are no records of a spouse for Kamimusubi, but several children are mentioned. === Children === *[[Ame-no-Koyane]]{{Cite web|first=|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto: Kami in Classic Texts: Amenokoyane|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=37|access-date=2021-03-27|website=Kokugakuin University|publisher=Establishment of a National Learning Institute for the Dissemination of Research on Shinto and Japanese Culture}}{{Cite book|last=Tanaka|title=Kashima Daiguji Keizu, Shokeifu「鹿嶋大宮司系図」『諸系譜』|volume=1|location=Japan}} *{{ill|Kisagaihime and Umugihime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ}} * [[Sukunabikona]] *Amenoikutama ===Family tree=== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} == See also == * [[Musuhi]] * [[Family tree of Japanese deities]] == References == {{Japanese mythology}} [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Japanese mythology]] [[Category:Japanese goddesses]] [[Category:Amatsukami]] [[Category:Pages from wikipedia]] [[Category:pages from wikipedia]][[en:Kamimusubi]][[Category:pages from wikipedia]] [[en:Kamimusubi]]

revision.text:
{{Expand Japanese|topic=cult|カミムスビ|date=October 2021}} {{Infobox deity | type = Japanese | name = Kamimusubi | image = Japanese Primordial Deities-en.svg | caption = Creation of the world according to the {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]}}, showing the five primordial gods (''[[kotoamatsukami]]'') and the subsequent seven generations of deities (''[[kamiyonanayo]]'') | god_of = ''[[Kotoamatsukami]]'' | script_name = Japanese | script = 神産巣日神 | other_names = Kamimusuhi, Kamumusubi | cult_center = | symbols = | consort = | parents = None; self-generated | siblings = | children = * [[Ame-no-Koyane]] * {{ill|Kisagaihime and Umugihime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ}} * [[Sukunabikona]] * Amenoikutama | texts = {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]}}, ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', ''Izumo-no-kuni Fudoki'' }} '''Kamimusubi''' (神産巣日), also known as '''Kamimusuhi''' among other variants, is a ''[[kami]]'' and [[god of creation]] in [[Japanese mythology]]. They are a ''[[hitorigami]],'' and the third of the first three ''kami'' to come into existence (''[[Kotoamatsukami]]''),{{Cite book |last=Gibney |first=Frank |title=Britannica International Encyclopedia |publisher=TBS-BRITANNICA |oclc=704151705}} alongside [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] and [[Takamimusubi]], forming a trio at the beginning of all creation.{{Cite book |last1=Breen |first1=John |url=https://www.taylorfrancis.com/books/mono/10.4324/9781315027890/shinto-history-john-breen-mark-teeuwen |title=Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami |last2=Teeuwen |first2=Mark |year=2013 |pages=55 |doi=10.4324/9781315027890|isbn=9781136826979 }} The name is composed of kami, denoting deity, and musubi, meaning "effecting force of creation". == Mythology == At the time of the [[Japanese creation myth|creation of heaven and earth]], Kamimusubi was in [[Takamagahara]] next to [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] and [[Takamimusubi]]. In {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]}}, Kamimusubi is an ancestral god who sits in Takamagahara and assists the gods of Izumo, and is called "Mi-Oya" (honorfic name for ancestor) by other gods.{{Cite book |last=Miura |first=Sukeyuki |title=Fudoki no Sekai『風土記の世界』 |publisher=Iwanamishoten |year=2016 |isbn=9784004316046 |location=Japan |pages=161–177}} Kamimusubi became the ancestral god of the five grains (progenitors) after Kamimusubi transformed the grains produced from the body of [[Ukemochi|Ōgetsu-hime]], the goddess of food, who was killed by [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]], the god of storms.{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9381 |archive-url= |access-date=2021-09-24 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}} At the beginning of the text, it is said that Kamimusubi is a genderless ''[[hitorigami]]'', but when [[Ōkuninushi]] is killed by [[:ja:八十神|Yasogami]](八十神), his mother, [[:ja:刺国若比売|Sashikuniwakahime]](刺国若比売), makes a request to Kamimusubi. However, here, Kamimusubi is considered a [[goddess]] because Kisagaihime and Umugihime were healed by "mother's milk". Since the Izumo gods are not mentioned in ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', Kamimusubi exists only as a kinematic pair of Takamimusubi. In ''Izumo-no-kuni Fudoki'', Kamimusubi appears as the ancestor of the ''Tochi-gami'' (land gods) who appear as the origin of the name of [[:ja:島根半島|Shimane Peninsula]] . Many of the ''Tochi-gami,'' such as Kisagaihime and Umugihime, are goddesses, and it is thought that Kamimusubi is the mother goddess in the genealogy of a [[Matriarchy|matriarchal society]]. Kamimusubi is considered in many versions to be the mother of [[Sukunabikona]]. {{Cite book |last=Ashkenazi |first=Michael |title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology |publisher=ABC-CLIO |year=2003 |isbn=9781576074671}} {{Page needed|date=August 2021}} Sukunabikona would later assist [[Ōkuninushi]] with the development of the land. While being an ''[[Amatsukami]]'' ('Kami of heaven')'','' Kamimusubi has a strong connection to ''[[Kunitsukami]]'' ('Kami of land') in [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] tradition. Because of this, it is also theorized that Kamimusubi was a kami of the Izumo clan. == Analysis == In the early Heian period, [[Kogo Shūi]] describes Kamimusubi as the ancestor of the Ki clan. During the medieval and early modern periods, [[Motoori Norinaga]] in his [[Kojiki-den|commentary on the ''Kojiki'']] wrote that Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] are the “First Ancestors of heaven and earth, of the ''kami'', and of all existence”,Sasaki, Kiyoshi. "Amenominakanushi no Kami in Late Tokugawa Period Kokugaku". ''Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics''. Kokugakuin University. Retrieved 2022-12-03. placing greater importance on them over [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]]. This viewpoint challenged the belief of the Watari Priesthood, which was that [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] was more important than Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] because they were created first. His reasoning is that Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] are the two ''[[kami]]'' of production, and thus:
Each and every thing in the world, from heaven and earth to its manifold beings and phenomena, one and all, came to be from the productive spirit [''musubi'' ] of these two great ''kami'' of production, so that even though manifold ''kami'' are in the world, it is these [two] ''kami'' that are particularly esteemed for the blessed virtue [''mimegumi''] of their productive spirit. It is they, of all others, which should be worshiped with highest esteem.
[[Hirata Atsutane]] describes Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] in a similar way, as the "ultimate natural ancestral ''kami'' of human beings", portraying them as "our great natural parent deities", giving birth to [[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]]. == Genealogy == There are no records of a spouse for Kamimusubi, but several children are mentioned. === Children === *[[Ame-no-Koyane]]{{Cite web|first=|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto: Kami in Classic Texts: Amenokoyane|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=37|access-date=2021-03-27|website=Kokugakuin University|publisher=Establishment of a National Learning Institute for the Dissemination of Research on Shinto and Japanese Culture}}{{Cite book|last=Tanaka|title=Kashima Daiguji Keizu, Shokeifu「鹿嶋大宮司系図」『諸系譜』|volume=1|location=Japan}} *{{ill|Kisagaihime and Umugihime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ}} * [[Sukunabikona]] *Amenoikutama ===Family tree=== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} == See also == * [[Musuhi]] * [[Family tree of Japanese deities]] == References == {{Japanese mythology}} [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Japanese mythology]] [[Category:Japanese goddesses]] [[Category:Amatsukami]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[en:Kamimusubi]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[en:Kamimusubi]]

{{Expand Japanese|topic=cult|カミムスビ|date=October 2021}} {{Infobox deity | type = Japanese | name = Kamimusubi | image = Japanese Primordial Deities-en.svg | caption = Creation of the world according to the {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]}}, showing the five primordial gods (''[[kotoamatsukami]]'') and the subsequent seven generations of deities (''[[kamiyonanayo]]'') | god_of = ''[[Kotoamatsukami]]'' | script_name = Japanese | script = 神産巣日神 | other_names = Kamimusuhi, Kamumusubi | cult_center = | symbols = | consort = | parents = None; self-generated | siblings = | children = * [[Ame-no-Koyane]] * {{ill|Kisagaihime and Umugihime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ}} * [[Sukunabikona]] * Amenoikutama | texts = {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]}}, ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', ''Izumo-no-kuni Fudoki'' }} '''Kamimusubi''' (神産巣日), also known as '''Kamimusuhi''' among other variants, is a ''[[kami]]'' and [[god of creation]] in [[Japanese mythology]]. They are a ''[[hitorigami]],'' and the third of the first three ''kami'' to come into existence (''[[Kotoamatsukami]]''),{{Cite book |last=Gibney |first=Frank |title=Britannica International Encyclopedia |publisher=TBS-BRITANNICA |oclc=704151705}} alongside [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] and [[Takamimusubi]], forming a trio at the beginning of all creation.{{Cite book |last1=Breen |first1=John |url=https://www.taylorfrancis.com/books/mono/10.4324/9781315027890/shinto-history-john-breen-mark-teeuwen |title=Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami |last2=Teeuwen |first2=Mark |year=2013 |pages=55 |doi=10.4324/9781315027890|isbn=9781136826979 }} The name is composed of kami, denoting deity, and musubi, meaning "effecting force of creation". == Mythology == At the time of the [[Japanese creation myth|creation of heaven and earth]], Kamimusubi was in [[Takamagahara]] next to [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] and [[Takamimusubi]]. In {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]}}, Kamimusubi is an ancestral god who sits in Takamagahara and assists the gods of Izumo, and is called "Mi-Oya" (honorfic name for ancestor) by other gods.{{Cite book |last=Miura |first=Sukeyuki |title=Fudoki no Sekai『風土記の世界』 |publisher=Iwanamishoten |year=2016 |isbn=9784004316046 |location=Japan |pages=161–177}} Kamimusubi became the ancestral god of the five grains (progenitors) after Kamimusubi transformed the grains produced from the body of [[Ukemochi|Ōgetsu-hime]], the goddess of food, who was killed by [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]], the god of storms.{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9381 |archive-url= |access-date=2021-09-24 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}} At the beginning of the text, it is said that Kamimusubi is a genderless ''[[hitorigami]]'', but when [[Ōkuninushi]] is killed by [[:ja:八十神|Yasogami]](八十神), his mother, [[:ja:刺国若比売|Sashikuniwakahime]](刺国若比売), makes a request to Kamimusubi. However, here, Kamimusubi is considered a [[goddess]] because Kisagaihime and Umugihime were healed by "mother's milk". Since the Izumo gods are not mentioned in ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', Kamimusubi exists only as a kinematic pair of Takamimusubi. In ''Izumo-no-kuni Fudoki'', Kamimusubi appears as the ancestor of the ''Tochi-gami'' (land gods) who appear as the origin of the name of [[:ja:島根半島|Shimane Peninsula]] . Many of the ''Tochi-gami,'' such as Kisagaihime and Umugihime, are goddesses, and it is thought that Kamimusubi is the mother goddess in the genealogy of a [[Matriarchy|matriarchal society]]. Kamimusubi is considered in many versions to be the mother of [[Sukunabikona]]. {{Cite book |last=Ashkenazi |first=Michael |title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology |publisher=ABC-CLIO |year=2003 |isbn=9781576074671}} {{Page needed|date=August 2021}} Sukunabikona would later assist [[Ōkuninushi]] with the development of the land. While being an ''[[Amatsukami]]'' ('Kami of heaven')'','' Kamimusubi has a strong connection to ''[[Kunitsukami]]'' ('Kami of land') in [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] tradition. Because of this, it is also theorized that Kamimusubi was a kami of the Izumo clan. == Analysis == In the early Heian period, [[Kogo Shūi]] describes Kamimusubi as the ancestor of the Ki clan. During the medieval and early modern periods, [[Motoori Norinaga]] in his [[Kojiki-den|commentary on the ''Kojiki'']] wrote that Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] are the “First Ancestors of heaven and earth, of the ''kami'', and of all existence”,Sasaki, Kiyoshi. "Amenominakanushi no Kami in Late Tokugawa Period Kokugaku". ''Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics''. Kokugakuin University. Retrieved 2022-12-03. placing greater importance on them over [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]]. This viewpoint challenged the belief of the Watari Priesthood, which was that [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] was more important than Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] because they were created first. His reasoning is that Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] are the two ''[[kami]]'' of production, and thus:
Each and every thing in the world, from heaven and earth to its manifold beings and phenomena, one and all, came to be from the productive spirit [''musubi'' ] of these two great ''kami'' of production, so that even though manifold ''kami'' are in the world, it is these [two] ''kami'' that are particularly esteemed for the blessed virtue [''mimegumi''] of their productive spirit. It is they, of all others, which should be worshiped with highest esteem.
[[Hirata Atsutane]] describes Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] in a similar way, as the "ultimate natural ancestral ''kami'' of human beings", portraying them as "our great natural parent deities", giving birth to [[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]]. == Genealogy == There are no records of a spouse for Kamimusubi, but several children are mentioned. === Children === *[[Ame-no-Koyane]]{{Cite web|first=|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto: Kami in Classic Texts: Amenokoyane|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=37|access-date=2021-03-27|website=Kokugakuin University|publisher=Establishment of a National Learning Institute for the Dissemination of Research on Shinto and Japanese Culture}}{{Cite book|last=Tanaka|title=Kashima Daiguji Keizu, Shokeifu「鹿嶋大宮司系図」『諸系譜』|volume=1|location=Japan}} *{{ill|Kisagaihime and Umugihime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ}} * [[Sukunabikona]] *Amenoikutama ===Family tree=== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} == See also == * [[Musuhi]] * [[Family tree of Japanese deities]] == References == {{Japanese mythology}} [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Japanese mythology]] [[Category:Japanese goddesses]] [[Category:Amatsukami]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia37]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia37]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Kamimusubi]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia37]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Kamimusubi]]

Kamimusubi is considered in many versions to be the mother of [[Sukunabikona]]. {{Cite book |last=Ashkenazi |first=Michael |title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology |publisher=ABC-CLIO |year=2003 |isbn=9781576074671}} {{Page needed|date=August 2021}} Sukunabikona would later assist [[Ōkuninushi]] with the development of the land. While being an ''[[Amatsukami]]'' ('Kami of heaven')'','' Kamimusubi has a strong connection to ''[[Kunitsukami]]'' ('Kami of land') in [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] tradition. "Amenominakanushi no Kami in Late Tokugawa Period Kokugaku". ''Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics''. Kokugakuin University. Retrieved 2022-12-03. placing greater importance on them over [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]]. However, here, Kamimusubi is considered a [[goddess]] because Kisagaihime and Umugihime were healed by "mother's milk". In ''Izumo-no-kuni Fudoki'', Kamimusubi appears as the ancestor of the ''Tochi-gami'' (land gods) who appear as the origin of the name of [[:ja:島根半島|Shimane Peninsula]] . Many of the ''Tochi-gami,'' such as Kisagaihime and Umugihime, are goddesses, and it is thought that Kamimusubi is the mother goddess in the genealogy of a [[Matriarchy|matriarchal society]]. Kamimusubi is considered in many versions to be the mother of [[Sukunabikona]]. {{Cite book |last=Ashkenazi |first=Michael |title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology |publisher=ABC-CLIO |year=2003 |isbn=9781576074671}} {{Page needed|date=August 2021}} Sukunabikona would later assist [[Ōkuninushi]] with the development of the land. While being an ''[[Amatsukami]]'' ('Kami of heaven')'','' Kamimusubi has a strong connection to ''[[Kunitsukami]]'' ('Kami of land') in [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] tradition. Because of this, it is also theorized that Kamimusubi was a kami of the Izumo clan. == Analysis == In the early Heian period, [[Kogo Shūi]] describes Kamimusubi as the ancestor of the Ki clan. During the medieval and early modern periods, [[Motoori Norinaga]] in his [[Kojiki-den|commentary on the ''Kojiki'']] wrote that Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] are the “First Ancestors of heaven and earth, of the ''kami'', and of all existence”,Sasaki, Kiyoshi. "Amenominakanushi no Kami in Late Tokugawa Period Kokugaku". ''Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics''. Kokugakuin University. Retrieved 2022-12-03. placing greater importance on them over [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]]. This viewpoint challenged the belief of the Watari Priesthood, which was that [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] was more important than Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] because they were created first. His reasoning is that Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] are the two ''[[kami]]'' of production, and thus:
Each and every thing in the world, from heaven and earth to its manifold beings and phenomena, one and all, came to be from the productive spirit [''musubi'' ] of these two great ''kami'' of production, so that even though manifold ''kami'' are in the world, it is these [two] ''kami'' that are particularly esteemed for the blessed virtue [''mimegumi''] of their productive spirit. It is they, of all others, which should be worshiped with highest esteem.
[[Hirata Atsutane]] describes Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] in a similar way, as the "ultimate natural ancestral ''kami'' of human beings", portraying them as "our great natural parent deities", giving birth to [[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]]. == Genealogy == There are no records of a spouse for Kamimusubi, but several children are mentioned. === Children === *[[Ame-no-Koyane]]{{Cite web|first=|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto: Kami in Classic Texts: Amenokoyane|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=37|access-date=2021-03-27|website=Kokugakuin University|publisher=Establishment of a National Learning Institute for the Dissemination of Research on Shinto and Japanese Culture}}{{Cite book|last=Tanaka|title=Kashima Daiguji Keizu, Shokeifu「鹿嶋大宮司系図」『諸系譜』|volume=1|location=Japan}} *{{ill|Kisagaihime and Umugihime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ}} * [[Sukunabikona]] *Amenoikutama ===Family tree=== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} == See also == * [[Musuhi]] * [[Family tree of Japanese deities]] == References == {{Japanese mythology}} [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Japanese mythology]] [[Category:Japanese goddesses]] [[Category:Amatsukami]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia04]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia04]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Kamimusubi]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia04]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Kamimusubi]]

{{Expand Japanese|topic=cult|カミムスビ|date=October 2021}} {{Infobox deity | type = Japanese | name = Kamimusubi | image = Japanese Primordial Deities-en.svg | caption = Creation of the world according to the {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]}}, showing the five primordial gods (''[[kotoamatsukami]]'') and the subsequent seven generations of deities (''[[kamiyonanayo]]'') | god_of = ''[[Kotoamatsukami]]'' | script_name = Japanese | script = 神産巣日神 | other_names = Kamimusuhi, Kamumusubi | cult_center = | symbols = | consort = | parents = None; self-generated | siblings = | children = * [[Ame-no-Koyane]] * {{ill|Kisagaihime and Umugihime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ}} * [[Sukunabikona]] * Amenoikutama | texts = {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]}}, ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', ''Izumo-no-kuni Fudoki'' }} '''Kamimusubi''' (神産巣日), also known as '''Kamimusuhi''' among other variants, is a ''[[kami]]'' and [[god of creation]] in [[Japanese mythology]]. They are a ''[[hitorigami]],'' and the third of the first three ''kami'' to come into existence (''[[Kotoamatsukami]]''),{{Cite book |last=Gibney |first=Frank |title=Britannica International Encyclopedia |publisher=TBS-BRITANNICA |oclc=704151705}} alongside [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] and [[Takamimusubi]], forming a trio at the beginning of all creation.{{Cite book |last1=Breen |first1=John |url=https://www.taylorfrancis.com/books/mono/10.4324/9781315027890/shinto-history-john-breen-mark-teeuwen |title=Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami |last2=Teeuwen |first2=Mark |year=2013 |pages=55 |doi=10.4324/9781315027890|isbn=9781136826979 }} The name is composed of kami, denoting deity, and musubi, meaning "effecting force of creation". == Mythology == At the time of the [[Japanese creation myth|creation of heaven and earth]], Kamimusubi was in [[Takamagahara]] next to [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] and [[Takamimusubi]]. In {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]}}, Kamimusubi is an ancestral god who sits in Takamagahara and assists the gods of Izumo, and is called "Mi-Oya" (honorfic name for ancestor) by other gods.{{Cite book |last=Miura |first=Sukeyuki |title=Fudoki no Sekai『風土記の世界』 |publisher=Iwanamishoten |year=2016 |isbn=9784004316046 |location=Japan |pages=161–177}} Kamimusubi became the ancestral god of the five grains (progenitors) after Kamimusubi transformed the grains produced from the body of [[Ukemochi|Ōgetsu-hime]], the goddess of food, who was killed by [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]], the god of storms.{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9381 |archive-url= |access-date=2021-09-24 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}} At the beginning of the text, it is said that Kamimusubi is a genderless ''[[hitorigami]]'', but when [[Ōkuninushi]] is killed by [[:ja:八十神|Yasogami]](八十神), his mother, [[:ja:刺国若比売|Sashikuniwakahime]](刺国若比売), makes a request to Kamimusubi. However, here, Kamimusubi is considered a [[goddess]] because Kisagaihime and Umugihime were healed by "mother's milk". Since the Izumo gods are not mentioned in ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', Kamimusubi exists only as a kinematic pair of Takamimusubi. In ''Izumo-no-kuni Fudoki'', Kamimusubi appears as the ancestor of the ''Tochi-gami'' (land gods) who appear as the origin of the name of [[:ja:島根半島|Shimane Peninsula]] . Many of the ''Tochi-gami,'' such as Kisagaihime and Umugihime, are goddesses, and it is thought that Kamimusubi is the mother goddess in the genealogy of a [[Matriarchy|matriarchal society]]. Kamimusubi is considered in many versions to be the mother of [[Sukunabikona]]. {{Cite book |last=Ashkenazi |first=Michael |title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology |publisher=ABC-CLIO |year=2003 |isbn=9781576074671}} {{Page needed|date=August 2021}} Sukunabikona would later assist [[Ōkuninushi]] with the development of the land. While being an ''[[Amatsukami]]'' ('Kami of heaven')'','' Kamimusubi has a strong connection to ''[[Kunitsukami]]'' ('Kami of land') in [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] tradition. Because of this, it is also theorized that Kamimusubi was a kami of the Izumo clan. == Analysis == In the early Heian period, [[Kogo Shūi]] describes Kamimusubi as the ancestor of the Ki clan. During the medieval and early modern periods, [[Motoori Norinaga]] in his [[Kojiki-den|commentary on the ''Kojiki'']] wrote that Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] are the “First Ancestors of heaven and earth, of the ''kami'', and of all existence”,Sasaki, Kiyoshi. "Amenominakanushi no Kami in Late Tokugawa Period Kokugaku". ''Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics''. Kokugakuin University. Retrieved 2022-12-03. placing greater importance on them over [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]]. This viewpoint challenged the belief of the Watari Priesthood, which was that [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] was more important than Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] because they were created first. His reasoning is that Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] are the two ''[[kami]]'' of production, and thus:
Each and every thing in the world, from heaven and earth to its manifold beings and phenomena, one and all, came to be from the productive spirit [''musubi'' ] of these two great ''kami'' of production, so that even though manifold ''kami'' are in the world, it is these [two] ''kami'' that are particularly esteemed for the blessed virtue [''mimegumi''] of their productive spirit. It is they, of all others, which should be worshiped with highest esteem.
[[Hirata Atsutane]] describes Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] in a similar way, as the "ultimate natural ancestral ''kami'' of human beings", portraying them as "our great natural parent deities", giving birth to [[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]]. == Genealogy == There are no records of a spouse for Kamimusubi, but several children are mentioned. === Children === *[[Ame-no-Koyane]]{{Cite web|first=|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto: Kami in Classic Texts: Amenokoyane|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=37|access-date=2021-03-27|website=Kokugakuin University|publisher=Establishment of a National Learning Institute for the Dissemination of Research on Shinto and Japanese Culture}}{{Cite book|last=Tanaka|title=Kashima Daiguji Keizu, Shokeifu「鹿嶋大宮司系図」『諸系譜』|volume=1|location=Japan}} *{{ill|Kisagaihime and Umugihime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ}} * [[Sukunabikona]] *Amenoikutama ===Family tree=== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} == See also == * [[Musuhi]] * [[Family tree of Japanese deities]] == References == {{Japanese mythology}} [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Japanese mythology]] [[Category:Japanese goddesses]] [[Category:Amatsukami]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Kamimusubi]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Kamimusubi]]

revision.text:
{{Expand Japanese|topic=cult|カミムスビ|date=October 2021}} {{Infobox deity | type = Japanese | name = Kamimusubi | image = Japanese Primordial Deities-en.svg | caption = Creation of the world according to the {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]}}, showing the five primordial gods (''[[kotoamatsukami]]'') and the subsequent seven generations of deities (''[[kamiyonanayo]]'') | god_of = ''[[Kotoamatsukami]]'' | script_name = Japanese | script = 神産巣日神 | other_names = Kamimusuhi, Kamumusubi | cult_center = | symbols = | consort = | parents = None; self-generated | siblings = | children = * [[Ame-no-Koyane]] * {{ill|Kisagaihime and Umugihime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ}} * [[Sukunabikona]] * Amenoikutama | texts = {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]}}, ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', ''Izumo-no-kuni Fudoki'' }} '''Kamimusubi''' (神産巣日), also known as '''Kamimusuhi''' among other variants, is a ''[[kami]]'' and [[god of creation]] in [[Japanese mythology]]. They are a ''[[hitorigami]],'' and the third of the first three ''kami'' to come into existence (''[[Kotoamatsukami]]''),{{Cite book |last=Gibney |first=Frank |title=Britannica International Encyclopedia |publisher=TBS-BRITANNICA |oclc=704151705}} alongside [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] and [[Takamimusubi]], forming a trio at the beginning of all creation.{{Cite book |last1=Breen |first1=John |url=https://www.taylorfrancis.com/books/mono/10.4324/9781315027890/shinto-history-john-breen-mark-teeuwen |title=Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami |last2=Teeuwen |first2=Mark |year=2013 |pages=55 |doi=10.4324/9781315027890|isbn=9781136826979 }} The name is composed of kami, denoting deity, and musubi, meaning "effecting force of creation". == Mythology == At the time of the [[Japanese creation myth|creation of heaven and earth]], Kamimusubi was in [[Takamagahara]] next to [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] and [[Takamimusubi]]. In {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]}}, Kamimusubi is an ancestral god who sits in Takamagahara and assists the gods of Izumo, and is called "Mi-Oya" (honorfic name for ancestor) by other gods.{{Cite book |last=Miura |first=Sukeyuki |title=Fudoki no Sekai『風土記の世界』 |publisher=Iwanamishoten |year=2016 |isbn=9784004316046 |location=Japan |pages=161–177}} Kamimusubi became the ancestral god of the five grains (progenitors) after Kamimusubi transformed the grains produced from the body of [[Ukemochi|Ōgetsu-hime]], the goddess of food, who was killed by [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]], the god of storms.{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9381 |archive-url= |access-date=2021-09-24 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}} At the beginning of the text, it is said that Kamimusubi is a genderless ''[[hitorigami]]'', but when [[Ōkuninushi]] is killed by [[:ja:八十神|Yasogami]](八十神), his mother, [[:ja:刺国若比売|Sashikuniwakahime]](刺国若比売), makes a request to Kamimusubi. However, here, Kamimusubi is considered a [[goddess]] because Kisagaihime and Umugihime were healed by "mother's milk". Since the Izumo gods are not mentioned in ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', Kamimusubi exists only as a kinematic pair of Takamimusubi. In ''Izumo-no-kuni Fudoki'', Kamimusubi appears as the ancestor of the ''Tochi-gami'' (land gods) who appear as the origin of the name of [[:ja:島根半島|Shimane Peninsula]] . Many of the ''Tochi-gami,'' such as Kisagaihime and Umugihime, are goddesses, and it is thought that Kamimusubi is the mother goddess in the genealogy of a [[Matriarchy|matriarchal society]]. Kamimusubi is considered in many versions to be the mother of [[Sukunabikona]]. {{Cite book |last=Ashkenazi |first=Michael |title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology |publisher=ABC-CLIO |year=2003 |isbn=9781576074671}} {{Page needed|date=August 2021}} Sukunabikona would later assist [[Ōkuninushi]] with the development of the land. While being an ''[[Amatsukami]]'' ('Kami of heaven')'','' Kamimusubi has a strong connection to ''[[Kunitsukami]]'' ('Kami of land') in [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] tradition. Because of this, it is also theorized that Kamimusubi was a kami of the Izumo clan. == Analysis == In the early Heian period, [[Kogo Shūi]] describes Kamimusubi as the ancestor of the Ki clan. During the medieval and early modern periods, [[Motoori Norinaga]] in his [[Kojiki-den|commentary on the ''Kojiki'']] wrote that Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] are the “First Ancestors of heaven and earth, of the ''kami'', and of all existence”,Sasaki, Kiyoshi. "Amenominakanushi no Kami in Late Tokugawa Period Kokugaku". ''Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics''. Kokugakuin University. Retrieved 2022-12-03. placing greater importance on them over [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]]. This viewpoint challenged the belief of the Watari Priesthood, which was that [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] was more important than Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] because they were created first. His reasoning is that Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] are the two ''[[kami]]'' of production, and thus:
Each and every thing in the world, from heaven and earth to its manifold beings and phenomena, one and all, came to be from the productive spirit [''musubi'' ] of these two great ''kami'' of production, so that even though manifold ''kami'' are in the world, it is these [two] ''kami'' that are particularly esteemed for the blessed virtue [''mimegumi''] of their productive spirit. It is they, of all others, which should be worshiped with highest esteem.
[[Hirata Atsutane]] describes Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] in a similar way, as the "ultimate natural ancestral ''kami'' of human beings", portraying them as "our great natural parent deities", giving birth to [[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]]. == Genealogy == There are no records of a spouse for Kamimusubi, but several children are mentioned. === Children === *[[Ame-no-Koyane]]{{Cite web|first=|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto: Kami in Classic Texts: Amenokoyane|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=37|access-date=2021-03-27|website=Kokugakuin University|publisher=Establishment of a National Learning Institute for the Dissemination of Research on Shinto and Japanese Culture}}{{Cite book|last=Tanaka|title=Kashima Daiguji Keizu, Shokeifu「鹿嶋大宮司系図」『諸系譜』|volume=1|location=Japan}} *{{ill|Kisagaihime and Umugihime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ}} * [[Sukunabikona]] *Amenoikutama ===Family tree=== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} == See also == * [[Musuhi]] * [[Family tree of Japanese deities]] == References == {{Japanese mythology}} [[category:Shinto kami]] [[category:Japanese mythology]] [[category:Japanese goddesses]] [[category:Amatsukami]] [[category:pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[category:pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[en:Kamimusubi]][[category:pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[en:Kamimusubi]]

"Amenominakanushi no Kami in Late Tokugawa Period Kokugaku". ''Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics''. Kokugakuin University. Retrieved 2022-12-03. placing greater importance on them over [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]]. In ''Izumo-no-kuni Fudoki'', Kamimusubi appears as the ancestor of the ''Tochi-gami'' (land gods) who appear as the origin of the name of [[:ja:島根半島|Shimane Peninsula]] . Many of the ''Tochi-gami,'' such as Kisagaihime and Umugihime, are goddesses, and it is thought that Kamimusubi is the mother goddess in the genealogy of a [[Matriarchy|matriarchal society]]. Kamimusubi is considered in many versions to be the mother of [[Sukunabikona]]. {{Cite book |last=Ashkenazi |first=Michael |title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology |publisher=ABC-CLIO |year=2003 |isbn=9781576074671}} {{Page needed|date=August 2021}} Sukunabikona would later assist [[Ōkuninushi]] with the development of the land. While being an ''[[Amatsukami]]'' ('Kami of heaven')'','' Kamimusubi has a strong connection to ''[[Kunitsukami]]'' ('Kami of land') in [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] tradition. Because of this, it is also theorized that Kamimusubi was a kami of the Izumo clan. == Analysis == In the early Heian period, [[Kogo Shūi]] describes Kamimusubi as the ancestor of the Ki clan. During the medieval and early modern periods, [[Motoori Norinaga]] in his [[Kojiki-den|commentary on the ''Kojiki'']] wrote that Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] are the “First Ancestors of heaven and earth, of the ''kami'', and of all existence”,Sasaki, Kiyoshi. "Amenominakanushi no Kami in Late Tokugawa Period Kokugaku". ''Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics''. Kokugakuin University. Retrieved 2022-12-03. placing greater importance on them over [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]]. This viewpoint challenged the belief of the Watari Priesthood, which was that [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] was more important than Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] because they were created first. His reasoning is that Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] are the two ''[[kami]]'' of production, and thus:
Each and every thing in the world, from heaven and earth to its manifold beings and phenomena, one and all, came to be from the productive spirit [''musubi'' ] of these two great ''kami'' of production, so that even though manifold ''kami'' are in the world, it is these [two] ''kami'' that are particularly esteemed for the blessed virtue [''mimegumi''] of their productive spirit. It is they, of all others, which should be worshiped with highest esteem.
[[Hirata Atsutane]] describes Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] in a similar way, as the "ultimate natural ancestral ''kami'' of human beings", portraying them as "our great natural parent deities", giving birth to [[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]]. == Genealogy == There are no records of a spouse for Kamimusubi, but several children are mentioned. === Children === *[[Ame-no-Koyane]]{{Cite web|first=|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto: Kami in Classic Texts: Amenokoyane|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=37|access-date=2021-03-27|website=Kokugakuin University|publisher=Establishment of a National Learning Institute for the Dissemination of Research on Shinto and Japanese Culture}}{{Cite book|last=Tanaka|title=Kashima Daiguji Keizu, Shokeifu「鹿嶋大宮司系図」『諸系譜』|volume=1|location=Japan}} *{{ill|Kisagaihime and Umugihime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ}} * [[Sukunabikona]] *Amenoikutama ===Family tree=== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} == See also == * [[Musuhi]] * [[Family tree of Japanese deities]] == References == {{Japanese mythology}} [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Japanese mythology]] [[Category:Japanese goddesses]] [[Category:Amatsukami]] [[Category:pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[en:Kamimusubi]][[Category:pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[en:Kamimusubi]]

revision.text:
{{Expand Japanese|topic=cult|カミムスビ|date=October 2021}} {{Infobox deity | type = Japanese | name = Kamimusubi | image = Japanese Primordial Deities-en.svg | caption = Creation of the world according to the {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]}}, showing the five primordial gods (''[[kotoamatsukami]]'') and the subsequent seven generations of deities (''[[kamiyonanayo]]'') | god_of = ''[[Kotoamatsukami]]'' | script_name = Japanese | script = 神産巣日神 | other_names = Kamimusuhi, Kamumusubi | cult_center = | symbols = | consort = | parents = None; self-generated | siblings = | children = * [[Ame-no-Koyane]] * {{ill|Kisagaihime and Umugihime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ}} * [[Sukunabikona]] * Amenoikutama | texts = {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]}}, ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', ''Izumo-no-kuni Fudoki'' }} '''Kamimusubi''' (神産巣日), also known as '''Kamimusuhi''' among other variants, is a ''[[kami]]'' and [[god of creation]] in [[Japanese mythology]]. They are a ''[[hitorigami]],'' and the third of the first three ''kami'' to come into existence (''[[Kotoamatsukami]]''),{{Cite book |last=Gibney |first=Frank |title=Britannica International Encyclopedia |publisher=TBS-BRITANNICA |oclc=704151705}} alongside [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] and [[Takamimusubi]], forming a trio at the beginning of all creation.{{Cite book |last1=Breen |first1=John |url=https://www.taylorfrancis.com/books/mono/10.4324/9781315027890/shinto-history-john-breen-mark-teeuwen |title=Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami |last2=Teeuwen |first2=Mark |year=2013 |pages=55 |doi=10.4324/9781315027890|isbn=9781136826979 }} The name is composed of kami, denoting deity, and musubi, meaning "effecting force of creation". == Mythology == At the time of the [[Japanese creation myth|creation of heaven and earth]], Kamimusubi was in [[Takamagahara]] next to [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] and [[Takamimusubi]]. In {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]}}, Kamimusubi is an ancestral god who sits in Takamagahara and assists the gods of Izumo, and is called "Mi-Oya" (honorfic name for ancestor) by other gods.{{Cite book |last=Miura |first=Sukeyuki |title=Fudoki no Sekai『風土記の世界』 |publisher=Iwanamishoten |year=2016 |isbn=9784004316046 |location=Japan |pages=161–177}} Kamimusubi became the ancestral god of the five grains (progenitors) after Kamimusubi transformed the grains produced from the body of [[Ukemochi|Ōgetsu-hime]], the goddess of food, who was killed by [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]], the god of storms.{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9381 |archive-url= |access-date=2021-09-24 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}} At the beginning of the text, it is said that Kamimusubi is a genderless ''[[hitorigami]]'', but when [[Ōkuninushi]] is killed by [[:ja:八十神|Yasogami]](八十神), his mother, [[:ja:刺国若比売|Sashikuniwakahime]](刺国若比売), makes a request to Kamimusubi. However, here, Kamimusubi is considered a [[goddess]] because Kisagaihime and Umugihime were healed by "mother's milk". Since the Izumo gods are not mentioned in ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', Kamimusubi exists only as a kinematic pair of Takamimusubi. In ''Izumo-no-kuni Fudoki'', Kamimusubi appears as the ancestor of the ''Tochi-gami'' (land gods) who appear as the origin of the name of [[:ja:島根半島|Shimane Peninsula]] . Many of the ''Tochi-gami,'' such as Kisagaihime and Umugihime, are goddesses, and it is thought that Kamimusubi is the mother goddess in the genealogy of a [[Matriarchy|matriarchal society]]. Kamimusubi is considered in many versions to be the mother of [[Sukunabikona]]. {{Cite book |last=Ashkenazi |first=Michael |title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology |publisher=ABC-CLIO |year=2003 |isbn=9781576074671}} {{Page needed|date=August 2021}} Sukunabikona would later assist [[Ōkuninushi]] with the development of the land. While being an ''[[Amatsukami]]'' ('Kami of heaven')'','' Kamimusubi has a strong connection to ''[[Kunitsukami]]'' ('Kami of land') in [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] tradition. Because of this, it is also theorized that Kamimusubi was a kami of the Izumo clan. == Analysis == In the early Heian period, [[Kogo Shūi]] describes Kamimusubi as the ancestor of the Ki clan. During the medieval and early modern periods, [[Motoori Norinaga]] in his [[Kojiki-den|commentary on the ''Kojiki'']] wrote that Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] are the “First Ancestors of heaven and earth, of the ''kami'', and of all existence”,Sasaki, Kiyoshi. "Amenominakanushi no Kami in Late Tokugawa Period Kokugaku". ''Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics''. Kokugakuin University. Retrieved 2022-12-03. placing greater importance on them over [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]]. This viewpoint challenged the belief of the Watari Priesthood, which was that [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] was more important than Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] because they were created first. His reasoning is that Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] are the two ''[[kami]]'' of production, and thus:
Each and every thing in the world, from heaven and earth to its manifold beings and phenomena, one and all, came to be from the productive spirit [''musubi'' ] of these two great ''kami'' of production, so that even though manifold ''kami'' are in the world, it is these [two] ''kami'' that are particularly esteemed for the blessed virtue [''mimegumi''] of their productive spirit. It is they, of all others, which should be worshiped with highest esteem.
[[Hirata Atsutane]] describes Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] in a similar way, as the "ultimate natural ancestral ''kami'' of human beings", portraying them as "our great natural parent deities", giving birth to [[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]]. == Genealogy == There are no records of a spouse for Kamimusubi, but several children are mentioned. === Children === *[[Ame-no-Koyane]]{{Cite web|first=|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto: Kami in Classic Texts: Amenokoyane|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=37|access-date=2021-03-27|website=Kokugakuin University|publisher=Establishment of a National Learning Institute for the Dissemination of Research on Shinto and Japanese Culture}}{{Cite book|last=Tanaka|title=Kashima Daiguji Keizu, Shokeifu「鹿嶋大宮司系図」『諸系譜』|volume=1|location=Japan}} *{{ill|Kisagaihime and Umugihime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ}} * [[Sukunabikona]] *Amenoikutama ===Family tree=== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} == See also == * [[Musuhi]] * [[Family tree of Japanese deities]] == References == {{Japanese mythology}} [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Japanese mythology]] [[Category:Japanese goddesses]] [[Category:Amatsukami]] [[Category:pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[en:Kamimusubi]][[Category:pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[en:Kamimusubi]]

Each and every thing in the world, from heaven and earth to its manifold beings and phenomena, one and all, came to be from the productive spirit [''musubi'' ] of these two great ''kami'' of production, so that even though manifold ''kami'' are in the world, it is these [two] ''kami'' that are particularly esteemed for the blessed virtue [''mimegumi''] of their productive spirit. It is they, of all others, which should be worshiped with highest esteem.
[[Hirata Atsutane]] describes Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] in a similar way, as the "ultimate natural ancestral ''kami'' of human beings", portraying them as "our great natural parent deities", giving birth to [[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]]. == Genealogy == There are no records of a spouse for Kamimusubi, but several children are mentioned. === Children === *[[Ame-no-Koyane]]{{Cite web|first=|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto: Kami in Classic Texts: Amenokoyane|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=37|access-date=2021-03-27|website=Kokugakuin University|publisher=Establishment of a National Learning Institute for the Dissemination of Research on Shinto and Japanese Culture}}{{Cite book|last=Tanaka|title=Kashima Daiguji Keizu, Shokeifu「鹿嶋大宮司系図」『諸系譜』|volume=1|location=Japan}} *{{ill|Kisagaihime and Umugihime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ}} * [[Sukunabikona]] *Amenoikutama ===Family tree=== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} == See also == * [[Musuhi]] * [[Family tree of Japanese deities]] == References == {{Japanese mythology}} [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Japanese mythology]] [[Category:Japanese goddesses]] [[Category:Amatsukami]] [[Category:Pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Kamimusubi]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Kamimusubi]][[Category:kk]] [[en:Kamimusubi]][[Category:Pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Kamimusubi]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[en:Kamimusubi]]

{{Expand Japanese|topic=cult|カミムスビ|date=October 2021}} {{Infobox deity | type = Japanese | name = Kamimusubi | image = Japanese Primordial Deities-en.svg | caption = Creation of the world according to the {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]}}, showing the five primordial gods (''[[kotoamatsukami]]'') and the subsequent seven generations of deities (''[[kamiyonanayo]]'') | god_of = ''[[Kotoamatsukami]]'' | script_name = Japanese | script = 神産巣日神 | other_names = Kamimusuhi, Kamumusubi | cult_center = | symbols = | consort = | parents = None; self-generated | siblings = | children = * [[Ame-no-Koyane]] * {{ill|Kisagaihime and Umugihime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ}} * [[Sukunabikona]] * Amenoikutama | texts = {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]}}, ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', ''Izumo-no-kuni Fudoki'' }} '''Kamimusubi''' (神産巣日), also known as '''Kamimusuhi''' among other variants, is a ''[[kami]]'' and [[god of creation]] in [[Japanese mythology]]. They are a ''[[hitorigami]],'' and the third of the first three ''kami'' to come into existence (''[[Kotoamatsukami]]''),{{Cite book |last=Gibney |first=Frank |title=Britannica International Encyclopedia |publisher=TBS-BRITANNICA |oclc=704151705}} alongside [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] and [[Takamimusubi]], forming a trio at the beginning of all creation.{{Cite book |last1=Breen |first1=John |url=https://www.taylorfrancis.com/books/mono/10.4324/9781315027890/shinto-history-john-breen-mark-teeuwen |title=Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami |last2=Teeuwen |first2=Mark |year=2013 |pages=55 |doi=10.4324/9781315027890|isbn=9781136826979 }} The name is composed of kami, denoting deity, and musubi, meaning "effecting force of creation". == Mythology == At the time of the [[Japanese creation myth|creation of heaven and earth]], Kamimusubi was in [[Takamagahara]] next to [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] and [[Takamimusubi]]. In {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]}}, Kamimusubi is an ancestral god who sits in Takamagahara and assists the gods of Izumo, and is called "Mi-Oya" (honorfic name for ancestor) by other gods.{{Cite book |last=Miura |first=Sukeyuki |title=Fudoki no Sekai『風土記の世界』 |publisher=Iwanamishoten |year=2016 |isbn=9784004316046 |location=Japan |pages=161–177}} Kamimusubi became the ancestral god of the five grains (progenitors) after Kamimusubi transformed the grains produced from the body of [[Ukemochi|Ōgetsu-hime]], the goddess of food, who was killed by [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]], the god of storms.{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9381 |archive-url= |access-date=2021-09-24 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}} At the beginning of the text, it is said that Kamimusubi is a genderless ''[[hitorigami]]'', but when [[Ōkuninushi]] is killed by [[:ja:八十神|Yasogami]](八十神), his mother, [[:ja:刺国若比売|Sashikuniwakahime]](刺国若比売), makes a request to Kamimusubi. However, here, Kamimusubi is considered a [[goddess]] because Kisagaihime and Umugihime were healed by "mother's milk". Since the Izumo gods are not mentioned in ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', Kamimusubi exists only as a kinematic pair of Takamimusubi. In ''Izumo-no-kuni Fudoki'', Kamimusubi appears as the ancestor of the ''Tochi-gami'' (land gods) who appear as the origin of the name of [[:ja:島根半島|Shimane Peninsula]] . Many of the ''Tochi-gami,'' such as Kisagaihime and Umugihime, are goddesses, and it is thought that Kamimusubi is the mother goddess in the genealogy of a [[Matriarchy|matriarchal society]]. Kamimusubi is considered in many versions to be the mother of [[Sukunabikona]]. {{Cite book |last=Ashkenazi |first=Michael |title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology |publisher=ABC-CLIO |year=2003 |isbn=9781576074671}} {{Page needed|date=August 2021}} Sukunabikona would later assist [[Ōkuninushi]] with the development of the land. While being an ''[[Amatsukami]]'' ('Kami of heaven')'','' Kamimusubi has a strong connection to ''[[Kunitsukami]]'' ('Kami of land') in [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] tradition. Because of this, it is also theorized that Kamimusubi was a kami of the Izumo clan. == Analysis == In the early Heian period, [[Kogo Shūi]] describes Kamimusubi as the ancestor of the Ki clan. During the medieval and early modern periods, [[Motoori Norinaga]] in his [[Kojiki-den|commentary on the ''Kojiki'']] wrote that Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] are the “First Ancestors of heaven and earth, of the ''kami'', and of all existence”,Sasaki, Kiyoshi. "Amenominakanushi no Kami in Late Tokugawa Period Kokugaku". ''Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics''. Kokugakuin University. Retrieved 2022-12-03. placing greater importance on them over [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]]. This viewpoint challenged the belief of the Watari Priesthood, which was that [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] was more important than Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] because they were created first. His reasoning is that Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] are the two ''[[kami]]'' of production, and thus:
Each and every thing in the world, from heaven and earth to its manifold beings and phenomena, one and all, came to be from the productive spirit [''musubi'' ] of these two great ''kami'' of production, so that even though manifold ''kami'' are in the world, it is these [two] ''kami'' that are particularly esteemed for the blessed virtue [''mimegumi''] of their productive spirit. It is they, of all others, which should be worshiped with highest esteem.
[[Hirata Atsutane]] describes Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] in a similar way, as the "ultimate natural ancestral ''kami'' of human beings", portraying them as "our great natural parent deities", giving birth to [[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]]. == Genealogy == There are no records of a spouse for Kamimusubi, but several children are mentioned. === Children === *[[Ame-no-Koyane]]{{Cite web|first=|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto: Kami in Classic Texts: Amenokoyane|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=37|access-date=2021-03-27|website=Kokugakuin University|publisher=Establishment of a National Learning Institute for the Dissemination of Research on Shinto and Japanese Culture}}{{Cite book|last=Tanaka|title=Kashima Daiguji Keizu, Shokeifu「鹿嶋大宮司系図」『諸系譜』|volume=1|location=Japan}} *{{ill|Kisagaihime and Umugihime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ}} * [[Sukunabikona]] *Amenoikutama ===Family tree=== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} == See also == * [[Musuhi]] * [[Family tree of Japanese deities]] == References == {{Japanese mythology}} [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Japanese mythology]] [[Category:Japanese goddesses]] [[Category:Amatsukami]] [[Category:Pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Kamimusubi]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Kamimusubi]][[Category:kk]] [[en:Kamimusubi]][[Category:Pages and categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Kamimusubi]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[en:Kamimusubi]]

Many of the ''Tochi-gami,'' such as Kisagaihime and Umugihime, are goddesses, and it is thought that Kamimusubi is the mother goddess in the genealogy of a [[Matriarchy|matriarchal society]]. Kamimusubi is considered in many versions to be the mother of [[Sukunabikona]]. {{Cite book |last=Ashkenazi |first=Michael |title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology |publisher=ABC-CLIO |year=2003 |isbn=9781576074671}} {{Page needed|date=August 2021}} Sukunabikona would later assist [[Ōkuninushi]] with the development of the land. Retrieved 2022-12-03. placing greater importance on them over [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]]. {{Expand Japanese|topic=cult|カミムスビ|date=October 2021}} {{Infobox deity | type = Japanese | name = Kamimusubi | image = Japanese Primordial Deities-en.svg | caption = Creation of the world according to the {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]}}, showing the five primordial gods (''[[kotoamatsukami]]'') and the subsequent seven generations of deities (''[[kamiyonanayo]]'') | god_of = ''[[Kotoamatsukami]]'' | script_name = Japanese | script = 神産巣日神 | other_names = Kamimusuhi, Kamumusubi | cult_center = | symbols = | consort = | parents = None; self-generated | siblings = | children = * [[Ame-no-Koyane]] * {{ill|Kisagaihime and Umugihime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ}} * [[Sukunabikona]] * Amenoikutama | texts = {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]}}, ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', ''Izumo-no-kuni Fudoki'' }} '''Kamimusubi''' (神産巣日), also known as '''Kamimusuhi''' among other variants, is a ''[[kami]]'' and [[god of creation]] in [[Japanese mythology]]. They are a ''[[hitorigami]],'' and the third of the first three ''kami'' to come into existence (''[[Kotoamatsukami]]''),{{Cite book |last=Gibney |first=Frank |title=Britannica International Encyclopedia |publisher=TBS-BRITANNICA |oclc=704151705}} alongside [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] and [[Takamimusubi]], forming a trio at the beginning of all creation.{{Cite book |last1=Breen |first1=John |url=https://www.taylorfrancis.com/books/mono/10.4324/9781315027890/shinto-history-john-breen-mark-teeuwen |title=Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami |last2=Teeuwen |first2=Mark |year=2013 |pages=55 |doi=10.4324/9781315027890|isbn=9781136826979 }} The name is composed of kami, denoting deity, and musubi, meaning "effecting force of creation". == Mythology == At the time of the [[Japanese creation myth|creation of heaven and earth]], Kamimusubi was in [[Takamagahara]] next to [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] and [[Takamimusubi]]. In {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]}}, Kamimusubi is an ancestral god who sits in Takamagahara and assists the gods of Izumo, and is called "Mi-Oya" (honorfic name for ancestor) by other gods.{{Cite book |last=Miura |first=Sukeyuki |title=Fudoki no Sekai『風土記の世界』 |publisher=Iwanamishoten |year=2016 |isbn=9784004316046 |location=Japan |pages=161–177}} Kamimusubi became the ancestral god of the five grains (progenitors) after Kamimusubi transformed the grains produced from the body of [[Ukemochi|Ōgetsu-hime]], the goddess of food, who was killed by [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]], the god of storms.{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9381 |archive-url= |access-date=2021-09-24 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}} At the beginning of the text, it is said that Kamimusubi is a genderless ''[[hitorigami]]'', but when [[Ōkuninushi]] is killed by [[:ja:八十神|Yasogami]](八十神), his mother, [[:ja:刺国若比売|Sashikuniwakahime]](刺国若比売), makes a request to Kamimusubi. However, here, Kamimusubi is considered a [[goddess]] because Kisagaihime and Umugihime were healed by "mother's milk". Since the Izumo gods are not mentioned in ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', Kamimusubi exists only as a kinematic pair of Takamimusubi. In ''Izumo-no-kuni Fudoki'', Kamimusubi appears as the ancestor of the ''Tochi-gami'' (land gods) who appear as the origin of the name of [[:ja:島根半島|Shimane Peninsula]] . Many of the ''Tochi-gami,'' such as Kisagaihime and Umugihime, are goddesses, and it is thought that Kamimusubi is the mother goddess in the genealogy of a [[Matriarchy|matriarchal society]]. Kamimusubi is considered in many versions to be the mother of [[Sukunabikona]]. {{Cite book |last=Ashkenazi |first=Michael |title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology |publisher=ABC-CLIO |year=2003 |isbn=9781576074671}} {{Page needed|date=August 2021}} Sukunabikona would later assist [[Ōkuninushi]] with the development of the land. While being an ''[[Amatsukami]]'' ('Kami of heaven')'','' Kamimusubi has a strong connection to ''[[Kunitsukami]]'' ('Kami of land') in [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] tradition. Because of this, it is also theorized that Kamimusubi was a kami of the Izumo clan. == Analysis == In the early Heian period, [[Kogo Shūi]] describes Kamimusubi as the ancestor of the Ki clan. During the medieval and early modern periods, [[Motoori Norinaga]] in his [[Kojiki-den|commentary on the ''Kojiki'']] wrote that Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] are the “First Ancestors of heaven and earth, of the ''kami'', and of all existence”,Sasaki, Kiyoshi. "Amenominakanushi no Kami in Late Tokugawa Period Kokugaku". ''Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics''. Kokugakuin University. Retrieved 2022-12-03. placing greater importance on them over [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]]. This viewpoint challenged the belief of the Watari Priesthood, which was that [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] was more important than Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] because they were created first. His reasoning is that Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] are the two ''[[kami]]'' of production, and thus:
Each and every thing in the world, from heaven and earth to its manifold beings and phenomena, one and all, came to be from the productive spirit [''musubi'' ] of these two great ''kami'' of production, so that even though manifold ''kami'' are in the world, it is these [two] ''kami'' that are particularly esteemed for the blessed virtue [''mimegumi''] of their productive spirit. It is they, of all others, which should be worshiped with highest esteem.
[[Hirata Atsutane]] describes Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] in a similar way, as the "ultimate natural ancestral ''kami'' of human beings", portraying them as "our great natural parent deities", giving birth to [[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]]. == Genealogy == There are no records of a spouse for Kamimusubi, but several children are mentioned. === Children === *[[Ame-no-Koyane]]{{Cite web|first=|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto: Kami in Classic Texts: Amenokoyane|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=37|access-date=2021-03-27|website=Kokugakuin University|publisher=Establishment of a National Learning Institute for the Dissemination of Research on Shinto and Japanese Culture}}{{Cite book|last=Tanaka|title=Kashima Daiguji Keizu, Shokeifu「鹿嶋大宮司系図」『諸系譜』|volume=1|location=Japan}} *{{ill|Kisagaihime and Umugihime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ}} * [[Sukunabikona]] *Amenoikutama ===Family tree=== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} == See also == * [[Musuhi]] * [[Family tree of Japanese deities]] == References == {{Japanese mythology}} [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Japanese mythology]] [[Category:Japanese goddesses]] [[Category:Amatsukami]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[en:Kamimusubi]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[en:Kamimusubi]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:dd]][[en:Kamimusubi]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[en:Kamimusubi]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:qq-]][[Category:qq]] [[en:Kamimusubi]]

Retrieved 2022-12-03. placing greater importance on them over [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]]. {{Expand Japanese|topic=cult|カミムスビ|date=October 2021}} {{Infobox deity | type = Japanese | name = Kamimusubi | image = Japanese Primordial Deities-en.svg | caption = Creation of the world according to the {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]}}, showing the five primordial gods (''[[kotoamatsukami]]'') and the subsequent seven generations of deities (''[[kamiyonanayo]]'') | god_of = ''[[Kotoamatsukami]]'' | script_name = Japanese | script = 神産巣日神 | other_names = Kamimusuhi, Kamumusubi | cult_center = | symbols = | consort = | parents = None; self-generated | siblings = | children = * [[Ame-no-Koyane]] * {{ill|Kisagaihime and Umugihime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ}} * [[Sukunabikona]] * Amenoikutama | texts = {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]}}, ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', ''Izumo-no-kuni Fudoki'' }} '''Kamimusubi''' (神産巣日), also known as '''Kamimusuhi''' among other variants, is a ''[[kami]]'' and [[god of creation]] in [[Japanese mythology]]. They are a ''[[hitorigami]],'' and the third of the first three ''kami'' to come into existence (''[[Kotoamatsukami]]''),{{Cite book |last=Gibney |first=Frank |title=Britannica International Encyclopedia |publisher=TBS-BRITANNICA |oclc=704151705}} alongside [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] and [[Takamimusubi]], forming a trio at the beginning of all creation.{{Cite book |last1=Breen |first1=John |url=https://www.taylorfrancis.com/books/mono/10.4324/9781315027890/shinto-history-john-breen-mark-teeuwen |title=Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami |last2=Teeuwen |first2=Mark |year=2013 |pages=55 |doi=10.4324/9781315027890|isbn=9781136826979 }} The name is composed of kami, denoting deity, and musubi, meaning "effecting force of creation". == Mythology == At the time of the [[Japanese creation myth|creation of heaven and earth]], Kamimusubi was in [[Takamagahara]] next to [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] and [[Takamimusubi]]. In {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]}}, Kamimusubi is an ancestral god who sits in Takamagahara and assists the gods of Izumo, and is called "Mi-Oya" (honorfic name for ancestor) by other gods.{{Cite book |last=Miura |first=Sukeyuki |title=Fudoki no Sekai『風土記の世界』 |publisher=Iwanamishoten |year=2016 |isbn=9784004316046 |location=Japan |pages=161–177}} Kamimusubi became the ancestral god of the five grains (progenitors) after Kamimusubi transformed the grains produced from the body of [[Ukemochi|Ōgetsu-hime]], the goddess of food, who was killed by [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]], the god of storms.{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9381 |archive-url= |access-date=2021-09-24 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}} At the beginning of the text, it is said that Kamimusubi is a genderless ''[[hitorigami]]'', but when [[Ōkuninushi]] is killed by [[:ja:八十神|Yasogami]](八十神), his mother, [[:ja:刺国若比売|Sashikuniwakahime]](刺国若比売), makes a request to Kamimusubi. However, here, Kamimusubi is considered a [[goddess]] because Kisagaihime and Umugihime were healed by "mother's milk". Since the Izumo gods are not mentioned in ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', Kamimusubi exists only as a kinematic pair of Takamimusubi. In ''Izumo-no-kuni Fudoki'', Kamimusubi appears as the ancestor of the ''Tochi-gami'' (land gods) who appear as the origin of the name of [[:ja:島根半島|Shimane Peninsula]] . Many of the ''Tochi-gami,'' such as Kisagaihime and Umugihime, are goddesses, and it is thought that Kamimusubi is the mother goddess in the genealogy of a [[Matriarchy|matriarchal society]]. Kamimusubi is considered in many versions to be the mother of [[Sukunabikona]]. {{Cite book |last=Ashkenazi |first=Michael |title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology |publisher=ABC-CLIO |year=2003 |isbn=9781576074671}} {{Page needed|date=August 2021}} Sukunabikona would later assist [[Ōkuninushi]] with the development of the land. While being an ''[[Amatsukami]]'' ('Kami of heaven')'','' Kamimusubi has a strong connection to ''[[Kunitsukami]]'' ('Kami of land') in [[Izumo Province|Izumo]] tradition. Because of this, it is also theorized that Kamimusubi was a kami of the Izumo clan. == Analysis == In the early Heian period, [[Kogo Shūi]] describes Kamimusubi as the ancestor of the Ki clan. During the medieval and early modern periods, [[Motoori Norinaga]] in his [[Kojiki-den|commentary on the ''Kojiki'']] wrote that Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] are the “First Ancestors of heaven and earth, of the ''kami'', and of all existence”,Sasaki, Kiyoshi. "Amenominakanushi no Kami in Late Tokugawa Period Kokugaku". ''Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics''. Kokugakuin University. Retrieved 2022-12-03. placing greater importance on them over [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]]. This viewpoint challenged the belief of the Watari Priesthood, which was that [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] was more important than Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] because they were created first. His reasoning is that Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] are the two ''[[kami]]'' of production, and thus:
Each and every thing in the world, from heaven and earth to its manifold beings and phenomena, one and all, came to be from the productive spirit [''musubi'' ] of these two great ''kami'' of production, so that even though manifold ''kami'' are in the world, it is these [two] ''kami'' that are particularly esteemed for the blessed virtue [''mimegumi''] of their productive spirit. It is they, of all others, which should be worshiped with highest esteem.
[[Hirata Atsutane]] describes Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] in a similar way, as the "ultimate natural ancestral ''kami'' of human beings", portraying them as "our great natural parent deities", giving birth to [[Izanagi]] and [[Izanami]]. == Genealogy == There are no records of a spouse for Kamimusubi, but several children are mentioned. === Children === *[[Ame-no-Koyane]]{{Cite web|first=|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto: Kami in Classic Texts: Amenokoyane|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=37|access-date=2021-03-27|website=Kokugakuin University|publisher=Establishment of a National Learning Institute for the Dissemination of Research on Shinto and Japanese Culture}}{{Cite book|last=Tanaka|title=Kashima Daiguji Keizu, Shokeifu「鹿嶋大宮司系図」『諸系譜』|volume=1|location=Japan}} *{{ill|Kisagaihime and Umugihime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ}} * [[Sukunabikona]] *Amenoikutama ===Family tree=== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} == See also == * [[Musuhi]] * [[Family tree of Japanese deities]] == References == {{Japanese mythology}} [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Japanese mythology]] [[Category:Japanese goddesses]] [[Category:Amatsukami]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]][[Category:qq 5]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[en:Kamimusubi]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]][[Category:qq 5]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[en:Kamimusubi]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:dd]][[en:Kamimusubi]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]][[Category:qq 5]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[en:Kamimusubi]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:qq-]][[Category:qq]] [[en:Kamimusubi]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]]
