They are a ''{{ill|Hitorigami|en|Hitorigami|WD=Q3138614|lt=Hitorigami|1=Hitorigami}},'' and the third of the first three ''kami'' to come into existence (''[[Kotoamatsukami]]''),{{Cite book |last=Gibney |first=FRank |title=Britannica International Encyclopedia |publisher=TBS-BRITANNICA |oclc=704151705}} alongside [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] and [[Takamimusubi]], forming a trio at the beginning of all creation.{{Cite book |last1=Breen |first1=John |url=https://www.taylorfrancis.com/books/mono/10.4324/9781315027890/shinto-history-john-breen-mark-teeuwen |title=Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami |last2=Teeuwen |first2=Mark |year=2013 |pages=55 |doi=10.4324/9781315027890|isbn=978dd36826979 }} The name is composed of kami, denoting deity, and musubi, meaning "effecting force of creation". == Mythology == At the time of the [[Japanese creation myth|creation of heaven and earth]], Kamimusubi was in [[Takamagahara]] next to [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] and [[Takamimusubi]]. In {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]}}, Kamimusubi is an ancestral god who sits in Takamagahara and assists the gods of Izumo, and is called "Mi-Oya" (honorfic name for ancestor) by other gods.{{Cite book |last=Miura |first=Sukeyuki |title=Fudoki no Sekai『風土記の世界』 |publisher=Iwanamishoten |year=2016 |isbn=9784004316046 |location=Japan |pages=161–177}} Kamimusubi became the ancestral god of the five grains (progenitors) after Kamimusubi transformed the grains produced from the body of {{ill|Ukemochi|en|Ukemochi|WD=Q2280323|lt=Ōgetsu-hime|1=Ukemochi}}, the goddess of food, who was killed by [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]], the god of storms.{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9381 |archive-url= |access-date=2021-09-24 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}} At the beginning of the text, it is said that Kamimusubi is a genderless ''{{ill|Hitorigami|en|Hitorigami|WD=Q3138614|lt=Hitorigami|1=Hitorigami}}'', but when [[Ōkuninushi]] is killed by [[:ja:八十神|Yasogami]](八十神), his mother, [[:ja:刺国若比売|Sashikuniwakahime]](刺国若比売), makes a request to Kamimusubi. However, here, Kamimusubi is considered a {{ill|Goddess|en|Goddess|WD=Q205985|lt=Goddess|1=Goddess}} because Kisagaihime and Umugihime were healed by "mother's milk". Since the Izumo gods are not mentioned in ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', Kamimusubi exists only as a kinematic pair of Takamimusubi. In ''Izumo-no-kuni Fudoki'', Kamimusubi appears as the ancestor of the ''Tochi-gami'' (land gods) who appear as the origin of the name of [[:ja:島根半島|Shimane Peninsula]] . Many of the ''Tochi-gami,'' such as Kisagaihime and Umugihime, are goddesses, and it is thought that Kamimusubi is the mother goddess in the genealogy of a [[Matriarchy|matriarchal society]]. Kamimusubi is considered in many versions to be the mother of [[Sukunabikona]]. {{Cite book |last=Ashkenazi |first=Michael |title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology |publisher=ABC-CLIO |year=2003 |isbn=9781576074671}} {{Page needed|date=August 2021}} Sukunabikona would later assist [[Ōkuninushi]] with the development of the land. While being an ''[[Amatsukami]]'' ('Kami of heaven')'','' Kamimusubi has a strong connection to ''[[Kunitsukami]]'' ('Kami of land') in {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|WD=Q582846|lt=Izumo|1=Izumo Province}} tradition. During the medieval and early modern periods, {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|WD=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga|1=Motoori Norinaga}} in his {{ill|Kojiki-den|en|Kojiki-den|WD=Q11410835|lt=commentary on the ''Kojiki''|1=Kojiki-den}} wrote that Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] are the “First Ancestors of heaven and earth, of the ''kami'', and of all existence”,Sasaki, Kiyoshi. "Amenominakanushi no Kami in Late Tokugawa Period Kokugaku". ''Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics''. Kokugakuin University. Retrieved 2022-12-03. placing greater importance on them over [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]]. This viewpoint challenged the belief of the Watari Priesthood, which was that [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] was more important than Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] because they were created first. His reasoning is that Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] are the two ''[[kami]]'' of production, and thus:
Each and every thing in the world, from heaven and earth to its manifold beings and phenomena, one and all, came to be from the productive spirit [''musubi'' ] of these two great ''kami'' of production, so that even though manifold ''kami'' are in the world, it is these [two] ''kami'' that are particularly esteemed for the blessed virtue [''mimegumi''] of their productive spirit. It is they, of all others, which should be worshiped with highest esteem.
[[Hirata Atsutane]] describes Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] in a similar way, as the "ultimate natural ancestral ''kami'' of human beings", portraying them as "our great natural parent deities", giving birth to [[Izanagi]] and {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami|1=Izanami}}. == Genealogy == There are no records of a spouse for Kamimusubi, but several children are mentioned. === Children === *[[Ame-no-Koyane]]{{Cite web|first=|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto: Kami in Classic Texts: Amenokoyane|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=37|access-date=2021-03-27|website=Kokugakuin University|publisher=Establishment of a National Learning Institute for the Dissemination of Research on Shinto and Japanese Culture}}{{Cite book|last=Tanaka|title=Kashima Daiguji Keizu, Shokeifu「鹿嶋大宮司系図」『諸系譜』|volume=1|location=Japan}} *{{ill|Kisagaihime and Umugihime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kisagaihime and Umugihime|1=Kisagaihime and Umugihime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11296639}} * [[Sukunabikona]] *Ame-no-Ikutama ===Family tree=== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} == See also == * [[Musubi]] * [[Family tree of Japanese deities]] == References == {{Japanese mythology}} [[en:Kamimusubi]] [[es:Kami-musubi-no-kami]] [[fr:Kamimusubi]] [[id:Kamimusubi]] [[ja:カミムスビ]] [[mni:ꯀꯃꯤꯃꯨꯁꯨꯕꯤ]] [[pl:Kamimusubi]] [[ru:Камимусуби-но ками]] [[sk:Kamimusubi]] [[sv:Kami-Musubi]] [[zh:神皇產靈尊]] {{wikidata link|Q3275901}} [[Category:Amatsukami]] [[Category:Japanese goddesses]] [[Category:Japanese mythology]] [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

{{ill|Q106267274|qid=Q106267274|1=Lady of the Agata Inukai clan (Wife of Fujiwara no Kuromaro)}}; {{ill|Q110379785|qid=Q110379785|1=Q110379785}} | venerated_in = {{ill|Japanese mythology|qid=Q814158|1=Japanese mythology}} }} '''Kamimusubi''' (神産巣日), also known as '''Kamimusuhi''' among other variants, is a ''[[kami]]'' and [[:en:God of creation|God of creation]] in [[Japanese mythology]]. They are a ''{{ill|Hitorigami|en|Hitorigami|qid=Q3138614|lt=Hitorigami|1=Hitorigami}},'' and the third of the first three ''kami'' to come into existence (''[[Kotoamatsukami]]''),{{Cite book |last=Gibney |first=FRank |title=Britannica International Encyclopedia |publisher=TBS-BRITANNICA |oclc=704151705}} alongside [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] and [[Takamimusubi]], forming a trio at the beginning of all creation.{{Cite book |last1=Breen |first1=John |url=https://www.taylorfrancis.com/books/mono/10.4324/9781315027890/shinto-history-john-breen-mark-teeuwen |title=Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami |last2=Teeuwen |first2=Mark |year=2013 |pages=55 |doi=10.4324/9781315027890|isbn=978dd36826979 }} The name is composed of kami, denoting deity, and musubi, meaning "effecting force of creation". == Mythology == At the time of the [[Japanese creation myth|creation of heaven and earth]], Kamimusubi was in [[Takamagahara]] next to [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] and [[Takamimusubi]]. In {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]}}, Kamimusubi is an ancestral god who sits in Takamagahara and assists the gods of Izumo, and is called "Mi-Oya" (honorfic name for ancestor) by other gods.{{Cite book |last=Miura |first=Sukeyuki |title=Fudoki no Sekai『風土記の世界』 |publisher=Iwanamishoten |year=2016 |isbn=9784004316046 |location=Japan |pages=161–177}} Kamimusubi became the ancestral god of the five grains (progenitors) after Kamimusubi transformed the grains produced from the body of {{ill|Ukemochi|en|Ukemochi|qid=Q2280323|lt=Ōgetsu-hime|1=Ukemochi}}, the goddess of food, who was killed by [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]], the god of storms.{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9381 |archive-url= |access-date=2021-09-24 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}} At the beginning of the text, it is said that Kamimusubi is a genderless ''{{ill|Hitorigami|en|Hitorigami|qid=Q3138614|lt=Hitorigami|1=Hitorigami}}'', but when [[Ōkuninushi]] is killed by [[:ja:八十神|Yasogami]](八十神), his mother, [[:ja:刺国若比売|Sashikuniwakahime]](刺国若比売), makes a request to Kamimusubi. However, here, Kamimusubi is considered a {{ill|Goddess|en|Goddess|qid=Q205985|lt=Goddess|1=Goddess}} because Kisagaihime and Umugihime were healed by "mother's milk". Since the Izumo gods are not mentioned in ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', Kamimusubi exists only as a kinematic pair of Takamimusubi. In ''Izumo-no-kuni Fudoki'', Kamimusubi appears as the ancestor of the ''Tochi-gami'' (land gods) who appear as the origin of the name of [[:ja:島根半島|Shimane Peninsula]] . Many of the ''Tochi-gami,'' such as Kisagaihime and Umugihime, are goddesses, and it is thought that Kamimusubi is the mother goddess in the genealogy of a [[Matriarchy|matriarchal society]]. Kamimusubi is considered in many versions to be the mother of [[Sukunabikona]]. {{Cite book |last=Ashkenazi |first=Michael |title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology |publisher=ABC-CLIO |year=2003 |isbn=9781576074671}} {{Page needed|date=August 2021}} Sukunabikona would later assist [[Ōkuninushi]] with the development of the land. While being an ''[[Amatsukami]]'' ('Kami of heaven')'','' Kamimusubi has a strong connection to ''[[Kunitsukami]]'' ('Kami of land') in {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|qid=Q582846|lt=Izumo|1=Izumo Province}} tradition. Because of this, it is also theorized that Kamimusubi was a kami of the Izumo clan. == Analysis == In the early Heian period, [[Kogo Shūi]] describes Kamimusubi as the ancestor of the Ki clan. During the medieval and early modern periods, {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|qid=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga|1=Motoori Norinaga}} in his {{ill|Kojiki-den|en|Kojiki-den|qid=Q11410835|lt=commentary on the ''Kojiki''|1=Kojiki-den}} wrote that Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] are the “First Ancestors of heaven and earth, of the ''kami'', and of all existence”,Sasaki, Kiyoshi. "Amenominakanushi no Kami in Late Tokugawa Period Kokugaku". ''Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics''. Kokugakuin University. Retrieved 2022-12-03. placing greater importance on them over [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]]. This viewpoint challenged the belief of the Watari Priesthood, which was that [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] was more important than Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] because they were created first. His reasoning is that Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] are the two ''[[kami]]'' of production, and thus:
Each and every thing in the world, from heaven and earth to its manifold beings and phenomena, one and all, came to be from the productive spirit [''musubi'' ] of these two great ''kami'' of production, so that even though manifold ''kami'' are in the world, it is these [two] ''kami'' that are particularly esteemed for the blessed virtue [''mimegumi''] of their productive spirit. It is they, of all others, which should be worshiped with highest esteem.
[[Hirata Atsutane]] describes Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] in a similar way, as the "ultimate natural ancestral ''kami'' of human beings", portraying them as "our great natural parent deities", giving birth to [[Izanagi]] and {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|qid=Q682306|lt=Izanami|1=Izanami}}. == Genealogy == There are no records of a spouse for Kamimusubi, but several children are mentioned. === Children === *[[Ame-no-Koyane]]{{Cite web|first=|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto: Kami in Classic Texts: Amenokoyane|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=37|access-date=2021-03-27|website=Kokugakuin University|publisher=Establishment of a National Learning Institute for the Dissemination of Research on Shinto and Japanese Culture}}{{Cite book|last=Tanaka|title=Kashima Daiguji Keizu, Shokeifu「鹿嶋大宮司系図」『諸系譜』|volume=1|location=Japan}} *{{ill|Kisagaihime and Umugihime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kisagaihime and Umugihime|1=Kisagaihime and Umugihime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11296639}} * [[Sukunabikona]] *Ame-no-Ikutama ===Family tree=== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} == See also == * [[Musubi]] * [[Family tree of Japanese deities]] == References == {{Japanese mythology}} [[en:Kamimusubi]] [[es:Kami-musubi-no-kami]] [[fr:Kamimusubi]] [[id:Kamimusubi]] [[ja:カミムスビ]] [[mni:ꯀꯃꯤꯃꯨꯁꯨꯕꯤ]] [[pl:Kamimusubi]] [[ru:Камимусуби-но ками]] [[sk:Kamimusubi]] [[sv:Kami-Musubi]] [[zh:神皇產靈尊]] {{wikidata link|Q3275901}} [[Category:Amatsukami]] [[Category:Japanese goddesses]] [[Category:Japanese mythology]] [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

{{Expand Japanese|topic=cult|カミムスビ|1=Kamimusubi|date=October 2021}} {{Infobox deity | type = Japanese | name = Kamimusubi | image = File:Kamimusubi shinbutsu-zue.jpg| caption = Creation of the world according to the {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]}}, showing the five primordial gods (''[[kotoamatsukami]]'') and the subsequent seven generations of deities (''[[kamiyonanayo]]'') | god_of = ''[[Kotoamatsukami]]'' | script_name = Japanese | script = 神産巣日神 | other_names = Kamimusuhi, Kamumusubi | cult_center = | symbols = | consort = | parents = None; self-generated | siblings = | children = * [[Ame-no-Koyane]] * {{ill|Kisagaihime and Umugihime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kisagaihime and Umugihime|1=Kisagaihime and Umugihime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11296639}} * [[Sukunabikona]] * Ame-no-Ikutama | texts = {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]}}, ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', ''Izumo-no-kuni Fudoki'' | gender = {{ill|intersex|qid=Q1097630|1=Intersex}}; {{ill|neutral sex|qid=Q52261234|1=neutral sex}} | offspring = {{ill|Futodama|qid=Q11059539|1=Futodama}}; {{ill|Kisagai-hime and Umugi-hime|qid=Q11296639|1=Kisagaihime and Umugihime}}; {{ill|Q108039939|qid=Q108039939|1=Kushimachi no Mikoto}}; {{ill|Q110064530|qid=Q110064530|1=Ame no Mikemochi}}; {{ill|Q97184246|qid=Q97184246|1=Ame-no-Ikutama}}; {{ill|Sukunabikona|qid=Q3275687|1=Sukunabikona}} | relatives = {{ill|Amanomichine|qid=Q10940767|1=Amanomichine}}; {{ill|Q106267274|qid=Q106267274|1=Lady of the Agata Inukai clan (Wife of Fujiwara no Kuromaro)}}; {{ill|Q110379785|qid=Q110379785|1=Q110379785}} | venerated_in = {{ill|Japanese mythology|qid=Q814158|1=Japanese mythology}} }} '''Kamimusubi''' (神産巣日), also known as '''Kamimusuhi''' among other variants, is a ''[[kami]]'' and [[:en:God of creation|God of creation]] in [[Japanese mythology]]. They are a ''{{ill|Hitorigami|en|Hitorigami|qid=Q3138614|lt=Hitorigami|1=Hitorigami}},'' and the third of the first three ''kami'' to come into existence (''[[Kotoamatsukami]]''),{{Cite book |last=Gibney |first=FRank |title=Britannica International Encyclopedia |publisher=TBS-BRITANNICA |oclc=704151705}} alongside [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] and [[Takamimusubi]], forming a trio at the beginning of all creation.{{Cite book |last1=Breen |first1=John |url=https://www.taylorfrancis.com/books/mono/10.4324/9781315027890/shinto-history-john-breen-mark-teeuwen |title=Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami |last2=Teeuwen |first2=Mark |year=2013 |pages=55 |doi=10.4324/9781315027890|isbn=978dd36826979 }} The name is composed of kami, denoting deity, and musubi, meaning "effecting force of creation". == Mythology == At the time of the [[Japanese creation myth|creation of heaven and earth]], Kamimusubi was in [[Takamagahara]] next to [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] and [[Takamimusubi]]. In {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Kojiki]]}}, Kamimusubi is an ancestral god who sits in Takamagahara and assists the gods of Izumo, and is called "Mi-Oya" (honorfic name for ancestor) by other gods.{{Cite book |last=Miura |first=Sukeyuki |title=Fudoki no Sekai『風土記の世界』 |publisher=Iwanamishoten |year=2016 |isbn=9784004316046 |location=Japan |pages=161–177}} Kamimusubi became the ancestral god of the five grains (progenitors) after Kamimusubi transformed the grains produced from the body of {{ill|Ukemochi|en|Ukemochi|qid=Q2280323|lt=Ōgetsu-hime|1=Ukemochi}}, the goddess of food, who was killed by [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]], the god of storms.{{Cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto詳細 |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9381 |archive-url= |access-date=2021-09-24 |website=國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |language=ja}} At the beginning of the text, it is said that Kamimusubi is a genderless ''{{ill|Hitorigami|en|Hitorigami|qid=Q3138614|lt=Hitorigami|1=Hitorigami}}'', but when [[Ōkuninushi]] is killed by [[:ja:八十神|Yasogami]](八十神), his mother, [[:ja:刺国若比売|Sashikuniwakahime]](刺国若比売), makes a request to Kamimusubi. However, here, Kamimusubi is considered a {{ill|Goddess|en|Goddess|qid=Q205985|lt=Goddess|1=Goddess}} because Kisagaihime and Umugihime were healed by "mother's milk". Since the Izumo gods are not mentioned in ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', Kamimusubi exists only as a kinematic pair of Takamimusubi. In ''Izumo-no-kuni Fudoki'', Kamimusubi appears as the ancestor of the ''Tochi-gami'' (land gods) who appear as the origin of the name of [[:ja:島根半島|Shimane Peninsula]] . Many of the ''Tochi-gami,'' such as Kisagaihime and Umugihime, are goddesses, and it is thought that Kamimusubi is the mother goddess in the genealogy of a [[Matriarchy|matriarchal society]]. Kamimusubi is considered in many versions to be the mother of [[Sukunabikona]]. {{Cite book |last=Ashkenazi |first=Michael |title=Handbook of Japanese Mythology |publisher=ABC-CLIO |year=2003 |isbn=9781576074671}} {{Page needed|date=August 2021}} Sukunabikona would later assist [[Ōkuninushi]] with the development of the land. While being an ''[[Amatsukami]]'' ('Kami of heaven')'','' Kamimusubi has a strong connection to ''[[Kunitsukami]]'' ('Kami of land') in {{ill|Izumo Province|en|Izumo Province|qid=Q582846|lt=Izumo|1=Izumo Province}} tradition. Because of this, it is also theorized that Kamimusubi was a kami of the Izumo clan. == Analysis == In the early Heian period, [[Kogo Shūi]] describes Kamimusubi as the ancestor of the Ki clan. During the medieval and early modern periods, {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|qid=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga|1=Motoori Norinaga}} in his {{ill|Kojiki-den|en|Kojiki-den|qid=Q11410835|lt=commentary on the ''Kojiki''|1=Kojiki-den}} wrote that Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] are the “First Ancestors of heaven and earth, of the ''kami'', and of all existence”,Sasaki, Kiyoshi. "Amenominakanushi no Kami in Late Tokugawa Period Kokugaku". ''Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics''. Kokugakuin University. Retrieved 2022-12-03. placing greater importance on them over [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]]. This viewpoint challenged the belief of the Watari Priesthood, which was that [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] was more important than Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] because they were created first. His reasoning is that Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] are the two ''[[kami]]'' of production, and thus:
Each and every thing in the world, from heaven and earth to its manifold beings and phenomena, one and all, came to be from the productive spirit [''musubi'' ] of these two great ''kami'' of production, so that even though manifold ''kami'' are in the world, it is these [two] ''kami'' that are particularly esteemed for the blessed virtue [''mimegumi''] of their productive spirit. It is they, of all others, which should be worshiped with highest esteem.
[[Hirata Atsutane]] describes Kamimusubi and [[Takamimusubi]] in a similar way, as the "ultimate natural ancestral ''kami'' of human beings", portraying them as "our great natural parent deities", giving birth to [[Izanagi]] and {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|qid=Q682306|lt=Izanami|1=Izanami}}. == Genealogy == There are no records of a spouse for Kamimusubi, but several children are mentioned. === Children === *[[Ame-no-Koyane]]{{Cite web|first=|title=Encyclopedia of Shinto: Kami in Classic Texts: Amenokoyane|url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=37|access-date=2021-03-27|website=Kokugakuin University|publisher=Establishment of a National Learning Institute for the Dissemination of Research on Shinto and Japanese Culture}}{{Cite book|last=Tanaka|title=Kashima Daiguji Keizu, Shokeifu「鹿嶋大宮司系図」『諸系譜』|volume=1|location=Japan}} *{{ill|Kisagaihime and Umugihime|ja|キサガイヒメ・ウムギヒメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kisagaihime and Umugihime|1=Kisagaihime and Umugihime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11296639}} * [[Sukunabikona]] *Ame-no-Ikutama ===Family tree=== {{Eight generations of Izumo}} == See also == * [[Musubi]] * [[Family tree of Japanese deities]] == References == {{Japanese mythology}} {{wikidata link|Q3275901|en|Kamimusubi|es|Kami-musubi-no-kami|fr|Kamimusubi|id|Kamimusubi|ja|カムムスヒ|mni|ꯀꯃꯤꯃꯨꯁꯨꯕꯤ|pl|Kamimusubi|ru|Камимусуби-но ками|sk|Kamimusubi|sv|Kami-Musubi|zh|神皇產靈尊|ja|カミムスビ}} [[Category:Amatsukami]] [[Category:Japanese goddesses]] [[Category:Japanese mythology]] [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with interwikis with duplicate languages]]
